item.title: SCP-Containment Breach tip
item.content:encoded: You can find a gas mask in 1123's cell, for any of you who play.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced, and thus is assumed to be non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). As such, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my opinion, the censoring of percentages and dates are unnecessary, especially in the conclusion section as well as in the early bits of the description, wherein only the last number of the year is censored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of the first few skips I read on this site and it actually made me want to start writing. All this time later, I still like this despite it's age showing a bit.

+1
And a well-deserved +200 at that.

item.title: Staff Post - Closed
item.content:encoded:
SCP-186
SCP-1981
SCP-1983

Look at that huh, three SCPs with political undertones (or, tones, depending on how you read them), proving that such articles can be popular and well-received on the site.

Additionally, denying a travesty and an awful event in human history is also contentious and quite frankly makes you look like, in the best-case scenario, an asshole. You don't get to get away with dropping such a statement in an SCP discussion thread, and I'm treating this the same as any other fool denying genocide on this site. So, you know, don't.

This is a closed staff post. Do not respond to this post for any reason if you are not staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep your politics out of my SCP. The Armenian "genocide" is a very contentious subject.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I know this was made in days of yore

Hence why it's allowed to stay as it is. The change has been reverted.

As a note, some authors prefer "amnesiacs" because that's the word that was used when the site started out. It's considered a stylistic choice, and up to the author to decide which they prefer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this was made in days of yore, but I changed "amnesiacs" to the more current and commonly accepted "amnestics." Please tell me if I did a bad. Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Test 2033

Date: ██/██/20██

Subject: White male, British. Age late 20s.

Procedure: Subject approaches SCP-1123 and is told to touch it.

Results: Subject reports a faint singing or “Chanting” sound and a mild scent of blood. Upon SCP-1123 subject ceases all movement and begins staring motionlessly upward at an approximately 60 degree angle. after 55 minutes of such a state subject begins ululating in a mixture of Latin, Koine Greek, and an unidentified language resembling Berber, before gradually collapsing into a fit of agonised screams (Note: Foundation linguists later confirmed the Latin and Greek portions to be derived from the Septuagint, the Greek translation of the Old Testament, including portions of the Psalms and the book of Isaiah). Subsequent interviews identify the imprint as that of a North African member of the 3rd Century Christian Church put to death under the Diocletianic persecutions in approximately 280 AD. Subject reports memories of praying amidst a group of other such individuals in the middle of a large amphitheatre, before being mauled to death by two lionesses."

You use my idea maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one SCP that would actually benefit humanity by being known. That it lets the silent majority be known, particularly that it's focus is the victims of genocide who otherwise could have slipped away unsung.

Additionally, I can see the benefit to the Foundation if they could reverse engineer it. A way for one body to hold multiple lifetimes worth of experience, if controllable, could be invaluable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well-written and I sort of like it. It feels slightly heavy-handed and overt in it's message, but it's forgivable since the article maintains a neutral tone and it's not implausible as an SCP.

It reminds me a lot of "The Inner Light" but still feels fresh enough of a take on the concept to get my upvote. This kind of stuff isn't much to my taste but what's well done is well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly. It is weird that all African mass slaughters, Rwanda genocide on top, were ignored.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one is simple, and has very little twist to it. It is presented as-is in a believable way, and has very straight forward rules that are easily understood. The origin, the mechanics of how it works, why it works, all these things are interpretable. I like this one for that reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The imprinted personality/memories only come from lives that ended before that of the subject. Past incarnations?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i like it, but having all four specific cases coming from europe and asia seemed a bit strange to me. Surely there's been plenty of horrific movements elsewhere. That's just me i guess tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should be noted that in treatment of these aftereffects, use of amnesiacs has not shown any psychological benefit, and has often proved to be harmful.

That last sentences seems, off to me.

Otherwise, I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Strange, there's no listing of any victims as a result of the Foundation. But then again, I guess the point of the Foundation IS to prevent massive fatalities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is my new favorite SCP. one of the few that actually gave me chills, it just pulled all the right strings for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. Pretty cliche. But pretty good, too. It would've been interesting if you would've included someone using a black light for testing to see if it would make them be able to see it from a farther distance, but still not work and be invisible, which would've made it a lot more interesting.

Even though it's cliche, it's good to see someone make a creepy ritual skull thing that isn't dangerous or possesses you or some stupid shit. So even though it's a creepy skull, it's not all that cliche. But maybe a little bit.

Anyways, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorite SCPs, possibly my favorite. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, ever since I started writing a paper on the Armenian Genocide, it just keeps on popping up. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs an experiment where someone that's already touched the skull touch it again after recovering. Do they get a refresher on the previous memory? Do they get a new person?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, it actually wasn't even Cambodian until late in the development. My original idea for this was going to use a Stalin era Soviet silent film.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …were you on IRC when I was talking about an SCP recovered from a Foundation facility in Phnom Penh the other week, or did you come up with this independently?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this could be improved with an imprint from before the 20th century.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now we need a D-class to be overwritten by another D-class, who was sacrificed in vain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, made that edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted this hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only quibble is the distance at which the writing is not visible should be specified as "beyond 5 meters". The wording as-is is a little unspecific and confusing.

Otherwise, this is just great. It speaks to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like that, used it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it over all, but the phrase "an actual human being" seems slightly off. Perhaps "a specific individual"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect not. There is some sort of association between the person touching and the person imprinted (as is implied by the fact that the imprint's life does not overlap the subject's, which is also why only recently we've had imprints postdating SCP-1123 1970's origin) just not one we're able to comprehend. Most likely, each imprint/subject pair is going to be unique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. No problem with typo fixes. And you got pretty much what I was going for here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a few bits of mixed up punctuation that look like they got missed. As for the article itself, I like it quite a bit. Then again, anything that points out a series of atrocities has a low percentage of reference because "only" 1 or 2 million innocent people were horribly killed, just kinda helps drive home what historically large dicks the human race can be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, especially the fact that it doesn't completely overwrite the person's psyche. Reminds me of the Star Trek episode where Picard got an entire life downloaded into his head.

I wonder what would happen if two people touched it at the same time? Would they get the same memory?

I modified a clunky piece of grammar in the first paragraph. If you don't like it, please change it back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well this is pretty straightforward. Downvoted to the lack of a hook and just nothing to keep me interested.

Good work on the tone and pacing though, keep that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I respectfully disagree on that point. There were a couple of reasons I put it there. Partly to show that there was a statistical logic to it, partly to imply how many tests they've done with this thing. Also, the last line is probably the most important of the the whole thing, ending with "where deaths are only estimated in the 1 to 2 million range." That's typical Foundation detachment, but the fridge horror should kick in when you realize that's not only on the low end, for events the average person's barely heard about if at all, but it at most accounts for 9% of the total, and probably closer to 1%.

That's what I was trying to do, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't seem much purpose to the quotations, so I removed them and changed the verbiage slightly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doesn't do much to me… seems like a milder SCP-911, in the sense that at least in this one the victims get their own memories back.

Test log also seems unnecessary, just repeating info already found in the article itself; the conclusion could be added as a simple addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I'm annoyed at the beginning where you quote people's sensations. A quote means you lifted it right from their words. I can't image someone actually describing any sensation they experience as "glass splinters in the sole of the foot".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rereading it I have to agree, it's kind of assumed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please Remove the conclusions section completely

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got that one. See any more typos? (I'm a rotten proofreader.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Latest addition, fingers crossed that it does better than the last two :)

Tried to keep the tone in line with the subject matter, let me know if it slipped anywhere.

Also, does anyone know the Khmer language? I used an online translator for the script on the skull, but I'd feel better if a human could confirm it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, couple spelling things (I assume in the final addendum you mean "probability" and not "probably"
